title: Isotopically enriched epitaxial CaWO$_{4}$ thin films for Er$^{3+}$ spin-photon quantum interfaces
authors: Hanlin Tang, Kidae Shin, Ashwin K. Boddeti, Sebastian P. Horvath, Adam Turflinger, Joseph Alexander, Jeffrey A. Dhas, Zihua Zhu, Shuhang Pan, Jeff D. Thompson, Yingge Du, Frederick J. Walker, Charles H. Ahn
abstract: Rare earth ion (REI)-doped oxide thin films are attractive for the application of quantum interconnects due to their stable optical levels and scalability$^{1-3}$. Among them, Er$^{3+}$ doped CaWO$_{4}$ is promising because it possesses narrow optical linewidth transitions and a long spin coherence time$^{4-6}$. The electron spin coherence is limited at high temperatures by paramagnetic impurities and by the presence of the 14.3% $^{183}$W nuclear spin. To further increase the spin coherence time at millikelvin temperatures, where the paramagnetic impurities are frozen out, our approach is to synthesize chemically and isotopically purified thin films as a host material. We first grow non-isotopically enriched Er$^{3+}$ doped CaWO$_{4}$ thin films, which exhibit a 214(13) MHz photoluminescence (PL) inhomogeneous linewidth, indicating the thin film has high crystalline quality. We then grow isotopically enriched CaWO$_{4}$ thin films using an isotopically purified $^{186}$WO$_{3}$ source. Time of flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF-SIMS) was used to measure the relative concentration of W isotopes. $^{183}$W, the only W isotope that has a net nuclear spin and is the major cause of spin decoherence, was at a relative abundance of 1.2%, a factor of 10 lower than natural abundance. We also observed PL emission from single ions after integrating nano-photonic devices with the thin film. These results establish isotopically engineered CaWO$_{4}$ thin films as a promising platform for future studies of nuclear-spin-limited coherence and for scalable rare-earth-ion-based quantum nanophotonic devices.
summary: This paper reports the successful synthesis of isotopically enriched Ca186WO4 thin films designed to improve the spin coherence of Er3+ ions. By reducing the concentration of the decohering 183W isotope from 14.3% to 1.2%, the authors provide a scalable platform for quantum interconnects. They demonstrate high crystalline quality and the ability to resolve single-ion resonances when integrated with nanophotonic cavities. While spectral diffusion was observed in non-stoichiometric films, the results establish a path toward high-performance, nuclear-spin-limited quantum interfaces.
main_problem: The spin coherence of Er3+ ions in CaWO4 is limited by the nuclear spin bath of the 14.3% natural abundance 183W isotope, hindering the development of scalable quantum interconnects.
main_result: The researchers successfully grew isotopically enriched Ca186WO4 thin films via MBE, reducing 183W abundance from 14.3% to 1.2% and demonstrating single-ion resonance features in integrated nanophotonic devices.
method: Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) was used for growth, with isotopic analysis performed via ToF-SIMS and optical characterization via photoluminescence and spectroscopy in integrated photonic crystal cavities.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of Er3+ ions implanted into epitaxial, isotopically engineered Ca186WO4 thin films integrated with silicon-on-insulator (SOI) 1D photonic crystal cavities.
key_observables: Inhomogeneous linewidth, 183W isotopic abundance, surface roughness, photoluminescence emission, and optical spectral diffusion.
important_parameters: 183W concentration (1.2%), 1.53 um telecom C-band transition, 214 MHz linewidth, and 20 mK operating temperature.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that reducing the 183W nuclear spin concentration will directly lead to increased spin coherence times (T2) by reducing the nuclear spin bath.
figures_summary: The figures demonstrate the successful growth of high-quality films, the reduction of 183W isotopes, the identification of spectral diffusion in off-stoichiometric films, and the resolution of individual Er3+ ion resonances in cavities.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of spin decoherence, describes the MBE growth and isotopic enrichment process, details the structural and isotopic characterization, discusses the optimization of film stoichiometry via annealing, and concludes with the demonstration of single-ion spectroscopy and identified limitations.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates a material engineering approach to controlling the environment (the nuclear spin bath) to extend the coherence of a quantum emitter.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
